ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Vietnamese savory sticky rice (Xoi Man). Closest parent is glutinous rice food product (FOODON_03315571).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (glutinous rice, cooked) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for Xoi Man with savory toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese savory sticky rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Xoi Man is approximately 250 g, representing a single-portion plate of savory sticky rice with toppings as commonly served in Vietnamese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Glutinous Rice, Cooked with Savory Toppings — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Glutinous Rice Cooked (NDB 20444) and component ingredient data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Xoi Man exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked glutinous rice (NDB 20444) as the base, with adjustments for typical savory toppings (pork floss, Chinese sausage, fried shallots, mung beans, soy sauce, sesame oil) based on standard Vietnamese Xoi Man composition. Energy and macros reflect a weighted composite of the rice base (~70% by weight) and toppings (~30% by weight).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Glutinous rice, cooked (white, sticky rice)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 86 is based on published GI values for cooked white glutinous (sticky) rice from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Xoi Man uses glutinous rice as its primary carbohydrate base; the savory toppings (protein and fat) may modestly reduce the effective GI of the composite dish, but the base GI of glutinous rice is used as the primary reference. GL per serving calculated as: (86 × 28.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 250g serving) / 100 = 61.3, rounded to 62.3 accounting for topping carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, Chinese sausage, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Xoi Man due to its high sodium content (approximately 320 mg per 100 g, equating to ~800 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents a significant proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index glutinous rice base causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Xoi Man with caution or avoid it, as the glutinous rice base has a high GI of approximately 86 and a high glycemic load per serving (~62), which can cause rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from Chinese sausage and pork toppings combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of Xoi Man due to its moderate saturated fat content from pork-based toppings and its high sodium content from soy sauce and processed meat ingredients, both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Xoi Man (Xôi mặn) is a traditional Vietnamese savory sticky rice dish made from steamed glutinous rice topped with a combination of savory ingredients such as pork floss (ruoc), Chinese sausage (lap cheong), fried shallots, steamed or split mung beans, and a drizzle of seasoned soy sauce and sesame oil. The glutinous rice is soaked overnight and then steamed until soft, sticky, and slightly translucent, giving it a characteristically chewy and dense texture. The toppings add layers of umami, saltiness, and richness that contrast with the mild sweetness of the rice base. Xoi Man is commonly eaten as a breakfast or snack dish in Vietnam and is also popular in Vietnamese diaspora communities in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-protein, and moderate-fat dish with a high glycemic index (~86) due to the glutinous rice base, making it a concern for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The dish is also relatively high in sodium (~320 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and processed meat toppings, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It provides moderate amounts of iron, zinc, and B vitamins from the rice and meat components. A standard serving is approximately 250 g and provides around 437 kcal.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in savory sticky rice?
Is sticky rice good for diabetics?

Where can I find Xoi Man in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Xoi Man was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked glutinous rice as the base with composite adjustments for typical savory toppings. GI is based on analogous glutinous rice data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct database match.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source database. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy glutinous rice entry used as the primary base. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite dish. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. Trans fat is 0.0 as glutinous rice and typical Xoi Man toppings do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA data. The cuisine field reflects the Vietnamese origin of this dish; it is served in Singapore by Vietnamese restaurants. The food was identified on field survey data from two Vietnamese field dataings (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant). Price data from field survey (SGD 14.90) is preserved as contextual metadata but not used for nutritional derivation. GL per serving = (86 × 28.5 / 100) × 250 = 61.3, reported as 62.3 to account for additional carbohydrate from toppings.
